questions.question: What does the acronym DEAR MAN stand for in DBT interpersonal effectiveness?

options.text: Defend, Explain, Argue, Resolve, Manage, Adapt, Negotiate
Describe, Express, Assert, Reinforce, stay Mindful, Appear confident, Negotiate
Discuss, Empathize, Accept, Rationalize, Motivate, Analyze, Neutralize
Define, Evaluate, Apologize, Reflect, Monitor, Agree, Normalize

questions.explanation: DEAR MAN stands for Describe (state facts), Express (share feelings), Assert (state what you need), Reinforce (explain positive outcomes), stay Mindful (stay focused on your goal), Appear confident (calm posture and voice), and Negotiate (be willing to give to get). It is used for objective effectiveness — getting what you want or need.

questions.question: Which type of interpersonal effectiveness should you prioritize when maintaining the relationship matters most?

options.text: Objective effectiveness using DEAR MAN
Self-respect effectiveness using FAST
Relationship effectiveness using GIVE
Distress tolerance using TIPP

questions.explanation: When preserving or strengthening the relationship is your highest priority, lead with GIVE (be Gentle, act Interested, Validate, use an Easy manner). This may mean not getting everything you want, but it keeps the relationship intact and the other person feeling respected.

questions.question: Which of the following is an interpersonal myth identified in DBT?

options.text: Conflict is a normal part of healthy relationships
If they really cared about me, they would know what I need without me asking
Saying no is a healthy boundary
My needs are as important as other people's needs

questions.explanation: The belief that others should automatically know what you need — 'If they really cared, they'd know' — is a common interpersonal myth. In reality, even people who love you cannot read your mind. Asking clearly is a sign of respect for the relationship, not a sign that it is failing.

questions.question: In the FAST skill, what does the 'A' stand for?

options.text: Always agree with the other person to avoid conflict
Act interested in the other person's perspective
Appear confident with steady eye contact and calm voice
No unnecessary apologies — only apologize when you have actually done something wrong

questions.explanation: In the FAST skill (used for self-respect effectiveness), the 'A' stands for no unnecessary Apologies. This means you should not apologize for having an opinion, making a request, or saying no. Apologize only when you have genuinely done something wrong. Chronic over-apologizing undermines your self-respect.

questions.question: What does DBT mean by 'validation' in interpersonal effectiveness?

options.text: Agreeing with everything the other person says to keep the peace
Proving that your own position is correct with evidence
Acknowledging the other person's feelings and perspective, even if you disagree with their conclusion
Asking a third party to confirm who is right in the disagreement

questions.explanation: Validation in DBT means acknowledging the other person's feelings, perspective, and effort — even when you disagree with their conclusion. You can validate someone's experience ('I can see why that upset you') without conceding your point. When people feel heard, they become much more willing to hear you in return.
